claimText: Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) technology enables direct brain-to-device communication by translating neural signals into commands, bypassing the need for muscular control.
evidenceExcerpt: BCI translates neural signals into commands, allowing device operation via thought alone, bypassing muscular control.
examUse: Statement-based question on the fundamental mechanism of BCI technology.
distractorHooks: BCI requires micro-muscular movements to operate devices
BCI translates vocal cord vibrations into digital commands

claimText: Brain activity for Brain-Computer Interfaces can be acquired using either invasive implants or non-invasive wearable devices.
evidenceExcerpt: Can use invasive implants or non-invasive wearables to acquire brain activity.
examUse: Statement-based question on the deployment methods of BCI.
distractorHooks: BCI technology exclusively requires invasive surgical implants
BCI can only be operated through non-invasive external helmets

claimText: Stanford scientists have developed a privacy-focused Brain-Computer Interface that requires a mental password before decoding thoughts.
evidenceExcerpt: Stanford developed a privacy-focused BCI requiring a mental password before decoding thoughts.
examUse: Current affairs statement on recent advancements in BCI privacy and security.
distractorHooks: BCI systems currently lack any form of password protection
The mental password BCI requires physical biometric verification

claimText: Applications of Brain-Computer Interfaces include restoring mobility for paralysis and stroke patients, speech restoration, mental wellness, and cognitive enhancement.
evidenceExcerpt: Applications include restoring mobility/speech, mental wellness, gaming, and cognitive enhancement.
examUse: Multiple-statement application question on the potential uses of BCI technology.
distractorHooks: BCI applications are strictly limited to medical rehabilitation
BCI cannot be used for cognitive enhancement or mental wellness
